hook: You already know that flipping a coin gives you a 50/50 chance — but what happens when situations get more complex, like drawing cards, winning games, or even measuring random darts thrown at a target? Basic intuition about chance can quickly break down without the right tools. This unit gives you those tools, turning uncertainty into something you can calculate, predict, and even visualize geometrically.
concept: Every topic in this unit is about quantifying uncertainty — figuring out how likely something is to happen by carefully counting and organizing possibilities. Together, these ideas form the foundation of probability theory, from simple sample spaces all the way to conditional reasoning and geometric models.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to list all possible outcomes of an experiment and use the ratio $P(E) = \frac{\text{favorable outcomes}}{\text{total outcomes}}$ to calculate basic probabilities.
You will distinguish between what probability predicts in theory and what actually happens in repeated trials, and explain why the two values get closer as experiments grow larger.
You will apply the Addition Rule to find the probability that event $A$ or event $B$ occurs, carefully accounting for any overlap between the two events.
You will determine whether two events affect each other and use the Multiplication Rule to calculate $P(A \text{ and } B)$ correctly in both cases.
You will calculate the probability that an event occurs given that another event has already happened, using the formula $P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(B)}$.
You will use permutations and combinations to efficiently count large sets of arrangements or selections, replacing tedious lists with powerful formulas like $\binom{n}{r}$.
You will calculate probabilities by comparing areas or lengths within a geometric figure, connecting your knowledge of geometry directly to the language of chance.
